title: Robotic Manipulation Fundamentals
objective: Understand how robots grasp and manipulate objects, including gripper design, grasp planning, force control, and the emerging role of machine learning in manipulation.
beginnerExplanation:
Think about how you pick up a glass of water. You look at the glass, judge its size and shape, reach toward it, open your hand, close your fingers around it with just enough force to hold it securely without crushing it, and lift. You do this effortlessly, yet it involves vision, motor planning, force sensing, and real-time feedback — all coordinated in milliseconds. Now try picking up a raw egg. You automatically adjust your grip force. Pick up a slippery wet bottle. You adjust again. This natural ability is extraordinarily difficult to replicate in a robot.

Robotic manipulation is the field concerned with how robots interact physically with objects — grasping, placing, pushing, sliding, inserting, and assembling. It is essential for manufacturing (pick-and-place, assembly, packaging), logistics (warehouse picking, palletising), agriculture (fruit harvesting), healthcare (surgical manipulation), and service robotics (cooking, cleaning, assisting).

The gripper is the robot's hand. The simplest gripper is a parallel-jaw gripper — two flat fingers that open and close. It is cheap, reliable, and works well for box-shaped objects. Vacuum grippers use suction cups to lift flat or smooth objects — ideal for moving boxes and sheet materials. Soft grippers made from flexible materials can conform to irregular shapes, making them popular for food handling and agricultural applications. Dexterous multi-fingered hands with 15+ joints can manipulate objects with human-like dexterity, but they are expensive, complex, and difficult to control.

Grasp planning is the process of deciding where and how to grip an object. For simple known objects, grasp points can be pre-programmed. For unknown objects, the robot uses vision to estimate the object's shape, identifies candidate grasp points, evaluates each candidate for stability (will the object slip?), and selects the best option. This is an active area of research, and deep learning is increasingly used to predict good grasp points directly from images or point clouds.
deeperExplanation:
Robotic manipulation involves several layers of complexity that distinguish it from simple pick-and-place. Force control is essential when the robot must interact with the environment rather than just move through free space. When a robot inserts a peg into a hole, pushes a button, or polishes a surface, it must control the forces it exerts, not just its position. Impedance control and admittance control are frameworks that regulate the relationship between the robot's motion and the forces at its end effector, allowing compliant behaviour — the robot 'gives' when it encounters resistance, rather than pushing through rigidly.

Grasp quality metrics quantify how good a particular grasp is. The force closure property ensures that the fingers can resist any external wrench (force and torque combination) applied to the object — if your grasp has force closure, the object will not slip or rotate out of your grip no matter what direction you push it. The grasp wrench space, computed from the contact points and friction cones, determines whether force closure is achieved. In practice, grasp planners evaluate many candidate grasps and select the one with the best quality metric.

Tactile sensing is the robot equivalent of touch. Force/torque sensors at the wrist measure aggregate forces, but tactile arrays on fingertips provide spatially distributed pressure information — critical for detecting slip (when an object starts sliding out of the grasp), estimating object properties (weight, stiffness, texture), and performing fine manipulation tasks like threading a bolt. Technologies include resistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, and optical tactile sensors. GelSight, a camera-based tactile sensor, provides remarkably detailed touch information by imaging the deformation of a soft gel pad.

Learning-based manipulation has made dramatic progress. Rather than analytically computing grasp points and motion plans, deep reinforcement learning and imitation learning allow robots to learn manipulation skills from experience or demonstration. Google's research labs have trained robots to grasp novel objects from jumbled bins with over 90% success by learning from hundreds of thousands of grasp attempts. However, learned policies can be brittle — they may fail on objects that differ significantly from the training distribution.

In Australian agriculture, robotic manipulation faces unique challenges. Harvesting robots must handle delicate produce — strawberries, tomatoes, apples — without bruising. Each fruit varies in size, shape, ripeness, and stem orientation. The harvesting robot must locate the fruit using vision, plan a grasp that avoids damage, detach the fruit from the plant (sometimes requiring a cutting action), and place it gently in a container. Companies working on this in Australia include the Queensland University of Technology's research groups and several agtech startups in the Lockyer Valley region.
keyTakeaways: Manipulation is one of the hardest unsolved problems in robotics — simple for humans, extremely difficult for machines
Gripper selection (parallel jaw, vacuum, soft, dexterous) depends on the objects being handled
Grasp planning evaluates candidate grasp points for stability and force closure
Force and impedance control enable robots to interact compliantly with the environment
Tactile sensing provides the touch feedback essential for robust grasping and fine manipulation
Deep learning is increasingly used for grasp prediction and manipulation skill learning
example: At a major Australian food processing facility, a robotic palletising cell uses a FANUC robot arm with interchangeable grippers. For cardboard cartons, a vacuum gripper with four suction cups lifts boxes at high speed — up to 25 boxes per minute. For irregular bags of frozen vegetables, a parallel-jaw gripper with padded fingers provides a more secure grasp. The robot uses a 3D vision system mounted above the conveyor to identify each product, determine its orientation, select the appropriate gripper (automatic tool change), plan a collision-free pick path, and place the product in the correct position on the pallet according to the stacking pattern. The force control system monitors grip force in real time — if a bag starts to slip (detected by a sudden force change), the gripper tightens. The system handles 15 different product types with minimal changeover, processing over 20,000 items per shift.
commonMistakes: Choosing a gripper based on the easiest object to handle rather than the hardest — the gripper must work for all objects in the application
Ignoring force control — a robot that only controls position will break things when it contacts the environment
Assuming grasp planning is solved — it works well for simple shapes but remains a major challenge for novel, deformable, or cluttered objects
Over-relying on learned grasping without understanding failure modes — learned policies can fail unpredictably on out-of-distribution objects
Forgetting about the rest of the manipulation pipeline — grasp execution, transport, and placement matter as much as the initial grasp
exercise: Design a simple robotic gripper on paper for a specific task: picking up tennis balls from a bucket and placing them into tubes. Sketch at least three different gripper concepts (parallel jaw, vacuum, soft/adaptive). For each concept, analyse: can it conform to the ball's curved surface? Can it handle balls at different orientations in the bucket? What happens if the ball is wet? How fast can it cycle? Select the best concept and explain your reasoning. Then research whether commercial solutions exist for this task — you may be surprised by the creative approaches used in real tennis ball packaging machines.

quiz.question: What is force closure in the context of robotic grasping?
options: The maximum force the gripper motor can produce
A grasp configuration where the fingers can resist any external force or torque on the object
Closing the gripper with maximum force to ensure the object does not move
A sensor that measures the closing force of the gripper
quiz.explanation: Force closure means that the set of contact forces the gripper can apply, considering friction, can resist any external wrench (combination of forces and torques) on the grasped object. A force-closure grasp ensures the object is fully constrained and will not slip or rotate regardless of external disturbances — it is the gold standard for grasp quality.

quiz.question: Why is impedance control important for manipulation tasks like inserting a peg into a hole?
options: It makes the robot move faster
It allows the robot to regulate the relationship between motion and force, enabling compliant behaviour during contact
It reduces the electrical impedance of the motor for better efficiency
It prevents the robot from moving during the insertion
quiz.explanation: Pure position control would cause the robot to exert dangerous forces if there is any misalignment between the peg and hole. Impedance control makes the robot behave like a spring-damper system — it allows small deviations from the commanded position in response to contact forces, enabling the peg to 'find' the hole through compliant interaction rather than brute-force positioning.

perspectives.applicationEngineer: As an application engineer, you design manipulation cells — selecting robots, grippers, vision systems, and programming the pick-and-place logic. You optimise for cycle time, reliability, and flexibility across product variants.
perspectives.researcher: As a manipulation researcher, you develop new grasping algorithms, tactile sensing approaches, and learning methods. You push the boundaries of what robots can handle — deformable objects, transparent objects, cluttered scenes.
perspectives.processEngineer: As a process engineer, you ensure the robotic manipulation system integrates smoothly into the production line — coordinating with upstream conveyor speeds, downstream packing requirements, and quality control checkpoints.
